content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 8, 1977

A-77-04-008

Dennis Omoto
Office of the City Attorney
City of Santa Monica
1685 Main Street
Santa Monica, CA 90401

Dear Mr. Omoto:

This letter will confirm the substance of our tele-
phone conversation of April 4, 1977, concerning the treatment
of loans made to candidates for elective office. In particular, we discussed the distinction between loans that are con-
tributions within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and
loans that are not contributions, and I outlined the factors
that are relevant to correctly characterizing a loan.

Initially, it should be noted that all loans to a
candidate (except those for personal purposes unrelated to
his candidacy or status as an officeholder)1/ must be reported
on a campaign statement regardless of whether they are contri-
butions.1 This is because Government Code Section 84210(g)
requires disclosure of all loans received, not only loans that
are contributions. Nevertheless, the correct characterization
of a loan as a contribution has various consequences and, there-
fore, is significant. For example, if a loan is a contribution
and the loan is for $5,000 or more, the lender will become a
"major donor" and will be required to file a campaign statement.
See Government Code Section 82013(c). If, on the other hand,

1/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(b)(1) provides that
any monetary or non-monetary payment to a candidate for which
full and adequate consideration is not made to the payor is a
contribution, "unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances
that the payment was received...for personal purposes unrelated
to his candidacy or status as an officeholder." In determining
which loans are reportable on a campaign statement this same
criterion is applicable, regardless of whether the loan is a
contribution.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322 5660 • EXECUTIVE-LEGAL 322 5901 • ENFORCEMENT COMPLAINT OFFICE 322

Dennis Omoto
Page Two
April 8, 1977

the loan is not a contribution, the lender will not incur a
filing obligation.

The decisive factor in determining whether a loan is
a contribution is whether the lender receives "full and adequate consideration" in exchange for the loan. This is because
under the Political Reform Act a payment made to a candidate
is a contribution "except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received by the payor." Government Code Section 82015; 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215. Thus, if a lender
receives full and adequate consideration in exchange for making a loan, he has not made a contribution.

In deciding whether full and adequate consideration
has been provided by the recipient of a "loan," it is important to keep in mind that full and adequate consideration is
a higher standard than the normal contract standard of "good
consideration" or "valuable consideration." Thus, it is not
sufficient for a candidate merely to promise to repay a loan
if he does not want the loan to be deemed a contribution. He
would have to provide consideration above and beyond his promise in order to satisfy the "full and adequate" standard.2/

Also, in applying the concept of "full and adequate
consideration" in the context of political campaigns, we have
developed certain guidelines, which I discussed with you on
the telephone. First, if a loan to a candidate is made by a
commercial lending institution, we generally will assume that
the loan is supported by full and adequate consideration and
will consider the loan a "contribution" only if the surrounding facts and circumstances indicate otherwise. Thus, if a
lending institution agreed to make a loan to a candidate on
terms more lenient than those that would apply to another person similarly situated to the candidate, we would consider
such a loan to be a contribution. More lenient terms might

2/ This high standard is necessary in order to make
the Act's definition of the term "contribution" comport with
the realities of politics. It is very common in the context
of political campaigns for a supporter to designate a payment
to a candidate a "loan" and for the candidate to promise to
repay the "loan," even though both parties realize that repayment is highly unlikely and do not, in fact, expect it to
occur. If a promise, which generally is good consideration
in the context of a contract, were considered "full and adequate consideration" in the campaign context, many transactions
which in reality involve a contribution would incorrectly and
misleadingly be characterized as loans.

Dennis Omoto
Page Three
April 8, 1977


include, for example, an interest rate lower than the going
rate or the absence of collateral when collateral ordinarily
would be required.

On the other hand, if a loan is made to a candidate by an individual who is not in the business of making
loans, we generally will assume that the loan is a contribution; and we will not indulge this assumption only if the
facts and circumstances clearly indicate that full adequate consideration has been provided. Furthermore, we
think that in a situation involving a loan from an individual
"full and adequate consideration" generally means that the
loan is supported by collateral, an interest rate equal to
or greater than the going rate, a payment schedule comparable
to that which would be imposed by a commercial lending institution, and a note signed by the candidate. In most instances,
only if these criteria are satisfied do we consider the loan
a genuine loan supported by full and adequate consideration,
and, hence, not a contribution.

One final point which we discussed is that Santa
Monica's campaign ordinance contains contribution limitations.
I indicated to you that the Political Reform Act only imposes
disclosure requirements and that our treatment of loans was
developed in this context. I further indicated that in the
context of contribution limitations, meaningful enforcement
of such limitations might require an even more inclusive
standard with respect to when a loan would be considered a
contribution.

I believe the foregoing accurately reflects the
substance of our conversation. If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Kenneth Finney
Chief
Legal Division

KF:jc

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 28, 1977

Mr. Dennis T. Omoto
Deputy City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
City Hall
Santa Monica, California

Dear Mr. Omoto:

Thank you for your letter of April 5, 1977 requesting an informal
opinion as to whether it is a conflict of interest for a member of the
City Architectural Review Board, who is a professional architect, to
represent a client under various specified circumstances.

In response to your first question, a City Architectural Review Board
member, who is a professional architect, may not appear on behalf of a
client before his own Board. Government Section 87100(f) provides:

"No public official at any level of state or local government
shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt
to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest."

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c) provides that:

"In any way attempting to use his or her official position
to influence a governmental decision" shall include furthering or attempting to affect in any matter any decision:

(1) Within or before his or her agency; or

(2) Before any agency which is appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency."

From your facts, we observe that the Board member is clearly a public
official within the meaning of Section 82048. Further, we assume that the
member has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87103 as the

1/ Unless otherwise indicated, all citations are to the California Government
Code.

Dennis T. Omoto
April 29, 1977
Page two

client is a source of income to the member. An appearance before the
member's own Board on behalf of a client falls squarely within the language
of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c)(i), cited above, and does constitute
an attempt to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision unless this activity comes within the exception contained in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(f), which, in pertinent part, provides:

"In any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision" shall not include:

(i) Appearances by a public official as a member of the
general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function to represent himself or herself on
matters related solely to his or her personal interests;"

One who appears before an agency on behalf of a client does not come within
this exception for two reasons. First, clearly, the appearance is not to
represent himself or herself but rather to represent the interests of
another, a client. Secondly, the representation is not on matters related
solely to his or her personal interest. To the contrary, the representation is on matters related to the interests of the client. While the
Board member has some business or commercial interest in providing this
professional representation, the appearance can hardly be said to be on
a matter related solely to the personal interest of the board member.

With regard to your second question, a City Architectural Review Board
member, who is a professional architect, may appear on behalf of a client
before the City Planning Commission. The above cited definition of
"attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision" covers only one's own agency or any agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency. As the City Planning Commission falls within neither of the categories, such an appearance would
not constitute "attempting to use his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision."

In response to your third question, the Commission has not interpreted
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c) to include any specific agencies, other
than those specified, within the definition. The regulation uses the
phrase "shall include" and then proceeds to list the most obvious factual
circumstances where abuse might occur. The regulation does not attempt
to provide a comprehensive listing of all factual circumstances which
might constitute "attempting to use his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision." This non-inclusive language in the regulation
would permit unique, though perhaps rare, factual circumstances not
covered in the regulation to be presented, which might constitute "attempting
to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision."

Dennis T. Omoto
April 29, 1977
page three

With regard to your fourth question, it has been staff's interpretation
that the clause "matters related solely to his or her personal interests"
excludes any matter in which the public official is representing a client's
interests or whether or not the public official might have a personal interest
as well. The word "solely" means "only". A public official appearing on
behalf of any interest other than his or her own personal interest is not
appearing on matters related "solely" to his or her personal interest.

In response to your last question, to date, the Commission has not
issued any opinion on the above question nor is there any other information
available to the Commission pertaining to this issue.

I hope that this informal advice will be of some assistance to you in
resolving the question before you.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Edgar A. Korry
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of Interest Division
916/322-6444

EAK:mfa
